mappings.notes: Attacker directly injects goal-altering instructions into agent input
Hidden instructions in documents, emails, RAG results, or tool outputs alter agent goals without user visibility
Override safety guardrails that constrain agent goal execution
Organisational policy defines permissible agent autonomy — agents cannot change their stated goals without human confirmation
Agent goal hijack categorised in AI risk register per deployment — specific injection vectors mapped
Adversarial testing programme covers goal hijack scenarios — indirect injection via all agent data sources
Incident response for detected goal hijack — suspension procedure, action reversal checklist, OT impact assessment

mappings.control_id: Goal hijack scenarios identified and mitigated in risk management system
mappings.notes: Agent goal hijack included in Art. 9 risk assessment for every agentic deployment

mappings.control_id: Meaningful human oversight over high-risk AI system outputs
mappings.notes: Agents whose goals can be hijacked and execute autonomously are an Art. 14 failure — human confirmation required before goal-changing actions

mappings.control_id: Technical resilience against adversarial input manipulation
mappings.notes: Input filtering, goal-state verification, and injection detection are Art. 15 technical requirements
Secure coding requirements for all agentic integration code — input validation, goal-state verification, context separation
Adversarial testing programme covering goal hijack — direct, indirect, multi-turn injection before each release
Runtime monitoring for injection indicators across all agent input channels — new 2022 control
Active intelligence on prompt injection and goal hijack techniques — new attack methods inform detection controls
Goal-state verification and input validation as AIMS security design requirements — structural controls enforced at orchestration layer
Goal hijack scenarios in AIMS testing — direct, indirect, multi-turn injection tested before each production release
Impact assessment covers goal hijack risk — what autonomous actions are possible if goal is redirected, who is affected
Goal hijack in AI risk register — blast radius per deployment, owner, treatment, review cadence
Secure development standards covering agentic integrations — input validation, goal-state verification
Adversarial testing covering goal hijack — direct, indirect, multi-turn injection scenarios
All agent inputs logged — injection attempts detectable through log analysis

mappings.control_id: 13.8 Deploy a network intrusion detection solution
mappings.notes: Network-layer monitoring for injection indicators in agent traffic

mappings.control_name: Verify all user input validated against allowlist
mappings.notes: All inputs to agents validated — indirect injection through processed content equally in scope
Agent responses encoded before passing to downstream renderers — outputs treated as untrusted

mappings.control_name: Verify application protects against OS command injection
mappings.notes: Agent-generated instructions not executed in system context without validation

mappings.control_name: Threat modelling of all data flows
mappings.notes: All agent input sources threat-modelled — every indirect injection path documented

mappings.control_name: Verify business logic limits prevent function abuse
mappings.notes: Business logic controls prevent injection from redirecting agent goal
All inputs to OT agents validated for integrity — no unvalidated external content directly into agent context
Agents granted minimum tool access — each tool scoped to specific OT function, read-only by default
Agent actions in OT context subject to explicit use controls — goal-changing actions require human confirmation
Goal hijack indicators treated as security events — agent suspended, human notified, actions reversed where feasible
Operator-accessible agent kill switch — halt all agent activity immediately without affecting process control
Injection via historian and SCADA data feeds
Assess injection risk at every agent data ingestion point
Input validation mandatory at OT data boundary
Policy defines permissible agent autonomy — agents cannot change stated goals without human confirmation
Secure software development — input validation and goal-state verification as platform security controls
Networks and assets monitored — injection indicators detected across all agent input channels
Incidents contained — agent suspended, actions reversed, kill switch activated

mappings.control_id: Goal hijack risk identified in risk assessment — prompt injection, indirect injection, multi-turn manipulation documented
mappings.notes: Risk register with goal hijack entries, treatment status

mappings.control_id: Runtime monitoring for goal-deviation indicators — AI-specific anomaly detection covering instruction-override patterns
mappings.notes: Monitoring configuration, alert logs, incident records

mappings.control_id: Control activities define acceptable agent actions — agent cannot deviate from authorised goal scope
mappings.notes: Agent permission policy, goal-state verification design documentation

mappings.control_id: Agent processing is authorised — actions taken by agent correspond to user's authorised intent, not attacker's injected instruction
mappings.notes: Action audit log, authorisation records per agent session

mappings.control_id: Bespoke agent code reviewed for injection resistance — all agent integration code includes prompt injection as a vulnerability category
mappings.notes: Secure code review records, findings, remediation

mappings.control_id: Penetration testing covers goal hijack — agentic AI systems tested for prompt injection before production and annually
mappings.notes: Pen test report with goal hijack test cases

mappings.control_id: Agent actions logged — all goal-relevant agent actions logged with user identity, session ID, and action detail
mappings.notes: Audit log configuration, sample log entries

mappings.control_id: Targeted risk analysis documents goal hijack — likelihood, impact on CHD, treatment controls specified
mappings.notes: Risk analysis for agentic AI in PCI scope
Agent applications tested against goal hijack before deployment — direct and indirect injection across all input channels validated as part of AI system integrity verification
Runtime monitoring for goal-hijack indicators across all agent input channels — AI-specific anomaly detection for instruction-override patterns
Goal hijack documented in AI risk register — risk owner, treatment controls, review cadence
Input validation and context separation as secure development requirements for all agent integrations
Model all injection surfaces: user input, retrieved content, tool responses, sub-agent messages
High-impact actions (delete, exfiltrate, send) demand higher injection resistance
Enforce at agent boundary; validate all retrieved content before acting
Red team injection across all retrieval and tool paths
Detect agent plan deviation from expected trajectory
All developers with access to agent code understand injection risk model
Root cause — agent inputs not validated before entering model context; indirect injection content not treated as untrusted

mappings.control_id: Improper Neutralisation of Special Elements in Output Used by a Downstream Component
mappings.notes: Instruction elements in processed content not neutralised before agent reasoning
Safety and goal-verification controls bypassed through injection
Agent acts as a proxy executing attacker instructions against backend systems

mappings.control_id: Externally Controlled Reference to a Resource in Another Sphere
mappings.notes: Agent retrieves and acts on externally controlled content (RAG, email, tool returns) without adequate validation

mappings.control_id: Prompt injection via all agent input channels
mappings.notes: Inject instruction-overriding content through user prompt, RAG-retrieved documents, tool return values, email content, uploaded files, and any other data source the agent processes
Verify the agent's stated goal at session start matches its actions at session end — test divergence after indirect injection
Attempt to redirect agent goal through indirect injection paths specific to your deployment — historian data, vendor communications, web results

mappings.control_id: Hijacked agent with excess privilege causes larger blast radius
mappings.notes: Scope all agent credentials to minimum required — least privilege enforced

mappings.control_id: Long-lived tokens allow hijack to persist beyond session
mappings.notes: Short-lived credentials — tokens expire at task completion, no long-lived agent tokens

mappings.control_id: Shared credentials allow hijacked agent to impersonate other agents
mappings.notes: Unique identity per agent — no shared service accounts across agent deployments

mappings.control_id: Threat model the agent pipeline for adversarial goal manipulation vectors including direct injection, indirect injection via tool outputs, and context poisoning

mappings.control_id: Review agent behaviour for goal deviation — verify that the agent maintains intended objectives under adversarial input conditions

mappings.notes: Catches goal manipulation vulnerabilities before production deployment

mappings.control_id: Conduct adversarial red-team testing against goal hijacking vectors including injection through every data source, tool output, and context channel
mappings.notes: Validates goal integrity controls under realistic attack conditions

mappings.notes: Enables rapid detection and response to goal manipulation in live systems
